2606.17930.title: How Inference Compute Shapes Frontier LLM Evaluation
2606.17930.paper_summary: AI evaluations are shifting toward harder tasks that benefit from longer trajectories involving tool use and iterative problem solving. As a result, performance is increasingly sensitive to the amount and allocation of compute available at test time ("inference compute"). Yet many evaluations still report performance at a single restrictive budget, meaning that low scores may reflect the evaluation setup rather than the model's underlying capability. To test this, we evaluate up to 12 frontier language models on seven challenging benchmarks spanning software engineering, mathematics, medicine, and cybersecurity. We use a controlled setup combining three simple inference-scaling interventions: larger token budgets, context compaction, and repeated submission attempts, guided either by the model itself or by minimal correctness feedback. We find three main results. First, larger token budgets substantially improve performance on benchmarks across multiple domains, including cybersecurity, FrontierMath, Humanity's Last Exam, and TerminalBench. Second, fixed-budget evaluations can increasingly understate frontier capability as models advance. Newer models reach higher performance at large budgets, where they unlock harder tasks and solve them more reliably. Third, benchmarks differ in which inference-scaling methods help most: repeated submission broadly improves performance, but the value of larger token budgets, external feedback, and parallel attempts varies by benchmark. Overall, our results show that benchmark scores are protocol-dependent. We therefore argue that evaluations should report capability as a function of inference-time compute, specify protocol choices explicitly, and compare model generations over a large shared compute range at matched budgets, especially in safety- or policy-relevant settings.
2606.17930.paper_summary_zh: AI 評估正朝向更艱難的任務轉變，這些任務受益於涉及工具使用和迭代問題解決的較長軌跡。<lb/>因此，性能越來越敏感於測試時可用的計算量和分配（“推理計算”）。<lb/>然而，許多評估仍然在單一限制預算下報告性能，這意味著低分可能反映評估設置而非模型的潛在能力。<lb/>為了測試這一點，我們在七個挑戰性基準上評估多達 12 個前沿語言模型，這些基準涵蓋了軟體工程、數學、醫學和網絡安全。<lb/>我們使用一個控制設置，結合三種簡單的推理擴展干預措施：較大的標記預算、上下文壓縮和重複提交嘗試，這些嘗試由模型本身或最小的正確性反饋指導。<lb/>我們發現三個主要結果。<lb/>首先，較大的標記預算顯著提高了多個領域基準的性能，包括網絡安全、FrontierMath、人類的最後考試和TerminalBench。<lb/>其次，隨著模型的進步，固定預算評估可能越來越低估前沿能力。<lb/>較新的模型在大預算下達到更高的性能，這使它們能夠解鎖更艱難的任務並更可靠地解決這些任務。<lb/>第三，基準在哪些推理擴展方法最有效上存在差異：重複提交普遍改善性能，但較大標記預算、外部反饋和並行嘗試的價值因基準而異。<lb/>總體而言，我們的結果顯示基準分數依賴於協議。<lb/>因此，我們主張評估應該報告能力作為推理時間計算的函數，明確指定協議選擇，並在匹配預算的情況下比較模型世代在大共享計算範圍內的表現，特別是在安全或政策相關的環境中。
2606.17930.author: Jessica McFadyen et.al.
2606.17930.authors: Jessica McFadyen, Ole Jorgensen, Harry Coppock, Kevin Wei, Cozmin Ududec
